data.title: Learn about Raiku: Solana's high-performance edge computing layer
data.description:
Modern blockchains have achieved scale but not finality. While Solana leads in throughput and latency, it still lacks reliable execution guarantees for high-stakes applications. Raiku introduces a programmable coordination layer that restores finality, predictability, and trust to on-chain systems without compromising performance. Built as a sidecar for the Solana validator network, Raiku enables developers to reserve block space in advance, precisely schedule transactions, and avoid all mempool clutter. This article explores how Raiku brings execution guarantees, MEV resistance, and pre-confirmation to Solana's monolithic architecture, providing a system focused not only on speed but also on reliability in a distributed adversarial world. The Current State of Blockchain Architecture and the Need for Deterministic Execution Global markets demand determinism. Cryptocurrencies often offer only promise. Today's blockchain infrastructure is riddled with architectural compromises. In pursuit of scale, we've built systems that violate core distributed computing principles, assuming unlimited bandwidth, ignoring latency bounds, and forcing applications to use common state models that they don't need. The result? Brittle abstractions, unreliable performance, and a developer experience that's more about survival than innovation. Imagine launching a modern financial product and being told your transaction might fail unless you spam, bribe validators, or simply pray for inclusion. Blockchain systems are ultimately supposed to abstract this complexity, providing efficient decentralized infrastructure. But in practice, we've replaced racks of servers with validator nodes, shifting latency concerns from physical hardware to Layer 1 congestion. This is the everyday reality of Web3. Ethereum, the most widely used smart contract platform, processes only 15-30 transactions per second. During periods of high demand, such as NFT swaps, liquidations, and memecoin season, the underlying network clogs and fees skyrocket, leaving Layer 2 as the only escape route. These Layer 2 Rollups offer massive throughput while reducing gas fees by moving execution off-chain, but they also introduce new trade-offs: fragmented liquidity, complex bridging, differentiated trust models, and a slightly flawed user experience. Meanwhile, Ethereum's restaking ecosystem introduces greater vulnerabilities. Protocols like EigenLayer require validators to leverage Ethereum's social consensus to protect third-party systems, effectively betting that the community will coordinate a rescue should any issues arise. Even Vitalik considers this approach highly risky. Ethereum's scaling remains a 10-year roadmap. Builders need to act now. Solana, on the other hand, pursues a high-performance monolithic design: it centralizes consensus and execution on a single chain, enabling extremely fast block times and parallel execution. In fact, Solana can routinely process 3,000-4,000 TPS on its global state machine, far exceeding Ethereum's throughput. This design enables low fees and near-instant finality under normal circumstances. However, Solana's single-chain architecture has also shown signs of strain under extreme load. During periods of usage surges, Solana can experience congestion and even outages as all dApps compete for the same global resources. For example, historically, sharp increases in traffic (such as from popular NFT minting or arbitrage bots) have led to block stalls and transaction failures. Notably, in February 2024, a bug triggered by the Solana runtime caused the network to halt for nearly five hours. Even when the network remains operational, users and bots often experience transaction failures or timeouts during peak demand, undermining Solana's otherwise strong user experience. In fact, high-frequency traders on Solana have observed transaction failure rates as high as 75% when the network is congested. This occurs because Solana's leader scheduling and QoS (Quality of Service) prioritization favor certain validators, and when too many transactions flood in, many transactions simply cannot be included in a timely manner. In addition to throughput limitations, both ecosystems face issues with MEV utilization and unpredictable execution. Despite its public leader scheduling mechanism, Solana still faces its own MEV dynamics. Solana's stake-weighted transaction scheduling mechanism means that large validators or order flow specialists can negotiate trades, prioritizing certain off-ledger transactions. This lack of transparency leads to centralization issues: well-connected bots or companies gain rapid access, while ordinary users struggle to process transactions. While Solana has introduced solutions like priority fees and Jito (a specialized MEV-aware client) to foster a more open fee market, the underlying problem remains: when the network is busy, inclusion becomes a bidding war or a game of insiders, leaving ordinary users facing uncertainty and transaction delays. From a state perspective, Ethereum's rollup-centric model fragments applications across various chains. Each rollup becomes an isolated island, requiring bridges, redundant tools, and additional security assumptions. When liquidity and contracts no longer coexist, the magic of DeFi—composability—is lost. What blockchains lack is scalability, the ability to avoid latency spikes, state fragmentation, or transaction failures. Blockchains still lack the precision required for global finance. This is the design space occupied by Raiku. Born out of years of frustration with Ethereum's infrastructure and a deep respect for simple system design, Raiku introduces a new type of infrastructure to Solana, centered not on more rollups but on precise execution. It extends Solana's core by providing a programmable building block—an edge computing environment—that gives applications dedicated fast lanes, guaranteed bandwidth, and deterministic settlement without having to manage separate chains or compromise composability. If Solana is the global highway, then Raiku is the intelligent traffic control system, providing fast lanes for the applications that need them most. It's not another rollup, nor is it a sidechain. Raiku is a rethinking of blockchain execution: fast, modular, programmable, and predictable. The system was built not to chase trends but to meet the needs of institutions, developers, and global-scale applications who demand certainty, not hope. From Constraints to Vision: The Origins of Raiku Raiku was born from a simple observation: not all blockchain workloads need to run in the same crowded kitchen. In late 2023, a group of researchers and Solana veterans began asking how to offload heavy computations from Solana's Layer 1 without losing on-chain advantage. Initially a research project (supported by the Super Team and the Solana Foundation), the initiative stemmed from their frustration with the stagnation of Ethereum-centric infrastructure. Ethereum's ecosystem, while rich in Layer 2s, hadn't achieved the leaps in performance or developer experience these researchers had hoped for. Rollups were making incremental progress but still relied on a relatively slow and congested base layer. In contrast, Solana represents an opportunity to try something different: its Layer 1 (L1) is already fast and proves that web-scale blockchains are possible, but it can also be improved through improvements to its architecture. The Raiku team noted that by January 2024, many Solana applications were "naturally gravitating" toward scaling architectures. In other words, projects were attempting to build their own mini-rollups or isolated execution layers to further scale the throughput of the base layer. Lacking an official framework, some teams attempted to repurpose tools like the Sovereign SDK (for independent chains) to create Solana extensions, or rollups. The results were less than ideal: misusing Ethereum-based rollup frameworks on Solana resulted in poor performance and significant friction. Each project rolling out its own extension led to state fragmentation (subject to essentially all of Ethereum's problems) and duplication of effort. Furthermore, these DIY solutions failed to integrate cleanly with Solana's design, encountering data throughput limitations, time constraints, and the inability to share state or accounts with Layer 1. This pattern highlights a clear design problem: Solana needs a purpose-built scaling framework, not one shoehorned in from elsewhere. The founding team of Raiku, led by Robin A. Nordnes and others, set out to address this problem by fundamentally building an "edge computing" layer tightly coupled to Solana. After investigating other new L1 layers, such as Aptos and Sui, they chose Solana, believing it was uniquely positioned to support their vision. Solana has a large user base, a strong developer community, and a solid architecture (often compared to the rapidly improving early days of Ethereum). Importantly, Solana's leadership and ecosystem are open to innovation, with core developers releasing updates every few weeks and enthusiastic about new scaling approaches. The idea wasn't to launch another independent L1 layer or create a completely separate shard, but rather to scale Solana in a complementary way. As one team member put it, "We're not L2, we're not L1...we're somewhere in between." More formally, Raiku positions itself as a block-building architecture, an infrastructure protocol that connects validators, applications, and the base chain to orchestrate high-performance execution, exceeding what the base layer itself can provide. If Solana L1 is the highway, Raiku builds the express lanes and traffic control systems, enabling certain applications to run faster and more predictably when needed. A key insight in Raiku's design is that many high-scale dApps don't need to constantly interact with the entire global state. Some applications can run largely independently (with their own order books or game engine logic), as long as they can occasionally settle to the main chain and leverage its security and liquidity when needed. As Nordnes explained, "most use cases with high-scale potential don't require state composability all the time." You can sandbox your application's execution in its own zone (e.g., Layer 2) while still gaining the mainchain's advantages of settlement finality, shared user accounts, on-chain price data, and asset security. This insight underpins Raiku's architecture: it strives to provide applications with their own sovereign execution environment (so they aren't bottlenecked by other environments) while maintaining the advantages of Solana's Layer 1 (a unified asset and identity space, and a high-performance settlement layer). In the Ethereum world, attempts could be made to achieve similar goals by launching Optimistic Rollup or ZK Rollup, but as the team points out, "You can build a Layer 2 in Ethereum, but you're still severely limited by the underlying Layer 1." Even the best Rollup is limited by Ethereum's data publishing throughput, latency, and upgrade timeline (fixing Layer 1 will take a decade). Rather than wait, the Raiku team saw an opportunity to leverage Solana's current strengths and innovate on top of them. Raiku will introduce a new layer running parallel to Solana consensus, operated by (and economically aligned with) the validator community, to coordinate advanced execution capabilities. Its vision is bold: leveraging Web2 and TradFi systems (think AWS or NASDAQ) to make on-chain applications "faster, more reliable, and more market-competitive" without compromising decentralization. By early 2024, this vision had gained widespread traction. Solana's scaling concept became a hot topic, and the community ultimately settled on using the term "network scaling" instead of "L2" to emphasize scaling on L1 rather than forking it. Raiku became one of the leading projects implementing this concept. Technically, scaling is similar to a rollup or sidechain, a label that perhaps understates the capabilities the Raiku team is building. Raiku adopted the term "edge computing" to describe its execution zone. The term "edge computing" is borrowed from traditional IT (referring to computing performed at the edge of the network, closer to where it's needed). Raiku's edge computing environment isn't a standalone blockchain competing with Solana, but rather a modular execution zone at the edge of the Solana network, purpose-built to handle specific workloads with deterministic performance. This term resonates with Web2 infrastructure teams and institutional players, bridging the conceptual gap between Rollups and the familiar edge servers. Essentially, Raiku provides custom Rollup-like environments (extensions) integrated into Solana's edge computing zone. This gives developers execution autonomy—the freedom to run their own logic and schedule without the hassle of launching an entirely new chain or distributing users across the ecosystem.

Redefining Rollups: From "Extensions" to "Edge Computing Environments"

It's worth noting that Raiku is reshaping the landscape of custom execution environments. In Solana circles, the term "extensions" is used to describe these Solana-customized rollups. However, the Raiku team believes the term is a bit narrow (and even stigmatized due to early, crude attempts). To capture the imagination of both cryptocurrency and traditional Web2 audiences, Raiku is building more than just scaling; they are building an “edge computing environment.” With Raiku’s edge computing environment, we no longer view the Layer 2 network as an external add-on on top of the Layer 1 network (L1), but as an integral part of the network architecture and a Layer 1 network ( Raiku's modular execution zones are an extension of Layer 1 (L1), located at the edge of the network, close to users and applications. Raiku calls these "modular execution zones," emphasizing that different modules (each module is an execution runtime/virtual machine) can be plugged into a unified system. These modular execution zones allow developers to plug different execution runtimes or custom virtual machines into a unified system, giving them unparalleled control over the underlying application logic. The Raiku team believes that performance at scale isn't learned, but built in from the ground up. Raiku starts where other technologies stop: by breaking through the physical boundaries of distributed networks, such as bandwidth, geography, and time constraints. Specifically, Raiku provides: System reliability that maintains robust operation even under extreme load and stress. Deterministic execution guarantees a predictable outcome for every transaction. By placing high-performance edge computing (HPEC) capabilities directly at the edge of the Solana network, low latency is achieved, enabling transaction processing in milliseconds. Developers can freely customize low-level logic, providing unparalleled flexibility and control. Raiku's coordination engine precisely coordinates transactions, ensuring they are quickly sent, scheduled, and confirmed, backed by a robust market for advance block space to guarantee transaction inclusion (more on this in a later section). Validator plugins support both ahead-of-time (AOT) and just-in-time (JIT) execution. Combined with streaming proofs, these plugins enable instant pre-confirmation of transactions, transforming today's unreliable, best-effort interactions into reliable and schedulable execution. Edge computing environments are truly meaningful. It helps to narratively differentiate Solana's approach from Ethereum's: Ethereum employs "Layer-2 Rollups," while Solana (via Raiku) employs "edge computing." The latter implies enhancement, not separation. It's a term that traditional finance understands. In enterprise computing, edge computing is a positive concept, implying faster response times by moving computation closer to where it's needed. Raiku effectively says: We're moving execution closer to the application (logically speaking), while still anchored to the mainnet. Therefore, throughout this report, we'll use the terms "edge computing environment," "extension," and "modular execution zone" interchangeably to reflect the specifics of Raiku's philosophy. Going forward, with the Raiku mainnet launch and increased market adoption, you may see "Raiku edge computing" become a branded term, much like Polkadot's "parachain" or Avalanche's "subnet." This terminology also makes it easier to express new features: for example, Raiku can say, "Deploy your own edge computing environment on Solana in a week," which sounds just like setting up a cloud environment and is therefore familiar to developers. By emphasizing "edge computing," Raiku aligns with a broader trend in web infrastructure: to increase speed, logic is moving closer to users (edge ​​networks, CDNs, etc.), except that "user" here refers to application transactions, and the edge refers to a special area within the network. This is a powerful analogy that helps more people understand how Raiku differs from ordinary scaling hacks. Raiku's design follows several key principles: Not all dApps require persistent global state: Some of the highest-throughput applications (exchanges, games, payment networks) can run in isolated environments for the majority of their activity, only using the main chain when needed. Raiku addresses this situation by providing opt-in isolation, freeing these applications from global mempool contention while still allowing them to access Layer-1 liquidity/state when necessary. This contrasts with the philosophy of Ethereum DeFi, where everything is highly intertwined on a single chain (a powerful philosophy, but unscalable when every small application requires global atomic composability). Raiku recognizes that temporal or contextual composability (only when needed) is sufficient in many cases, resulting in significant performance improvements. Maintaining a Single Network Feel: Despite introducing modular partitioning, Raiku strives to avoid the user experience challenges of multiple chains. Global accounts and an orchestration engine ensure that Solana remains a single network from a user perspective. There's no need to manage multiple tokens on different chains/partitions to pay for gas, nor is there any need to manually switch RPC endpoints. You interact with Solana, and behind the scenes, Raiku routes your transactions to an extension chain or the main chain, as appropriate. This is in stark contrast to the Cosmos application chain model, or even Ethereum's Layer-2 architecture, where adopting a new chain means a new token, a new block explorer, and a shift in mindset. Raiku's edge computing zones act more like "network extensions" than independent networks, suggesting they extend Solana rather than compete with it. The architectural advantage lies in the preservation of network effects: the utility of the SOL token remains supportive (fees, staking), and Solana's community isn't fragmented into dozens of smaller chains. This addresses a common criticism of Ethereum's rollup-centric roadmap: that Ethereum risks becoming merely a settlement layer while user activity migrates to various Layer-2 tokens and ecosystems, potentially weakening Ethereum's economic security. Raiku's approach increases capacity and brings it under the Solana economic umbrella. Leverage existing security, don't reinvent it: Raiku doesn't create a new underlying consensus mechanism or require users to delegate funds to a completely new set of validators (in fact, Raiku doesn't hold custody of funds; assets remain on Solana). This offers significant advantages over launching a self-hosted application chain or a new Layer-1. If a project chooses to launch its own chain today (whether through the Cosmos SDK, an Avalanche subnet, or some autonomous rollup), it faces the daunting task of bootstrapping validators, incentivizing them (typically through inflationary new token rewards), and securing bridges to other ecosystems. Raiku simplifies this process by building on Solana's validator community and bridging natively through a global account. There's no need for a separate bridge contract; the extension is a logical part of Solana. This significantly reduces security risk and development overhead compared to a sovereign chain approach. For example, some teams attempted to use the Sovereign SDK on Solana, ultimately leading to state fragmentation and poor performance because it wasn't designed for Solana's use case. Raiku's custom solution avoids these pitfalls and maximizes reuse of Solana's proven components (such as the network, validator incentives, etc.). Predictability and transparency as core features: Builders and users alike value knowing what will happen in the future. Raiku implements predictability at the protocol level. The inclusion of signaling eliminates guesswork from the transaction submission process. MEV's design makes it more efficient (there is no private mempool; all transactions are conducted through auctions or known channels). This fosters a healthier ecosystem. On Ethereum, despite improvements, users still worry about being targeted by arbitrage bots when sending Uniswap transactions. On Solana, users worry about transactions "not completing" when the network is busy. Raiku aims to eliminate these concerns and make blockchain feel, in its best sense, reliable and "boring," like AWS infrastructure—if you schedule a task, you trust it to run on time. This is a key selling point for both institutional adoption (which demands SLAs and predictability) and mass consumer use (no one wants to constantly send "submit" commands hoping a transaction will succeed). Real-World Use Cases Enabled by Raiku's Edge Computing Design What can developers actually do with Raiku that wasn't possible before? The answer: Build on-chain applications with the speed and guarantees of off-chain systems, and deploy new types of services on Solana that previously might have required separate chains or centralized solutions. Let's explore some illustrative use cases envisioned by the Raiku team and community, highlighting how an edge computing approach can be effective: High-Frequency Trading and Exchanges (Using Drift Protocol's Swift as an Example): Drift is a leading perpetual swap exchange built on Solana, processing massive trading volumes. In early 2025, they launched the Swift protocol, an on-chain, ultra-low-latency matching engine built directly on Solana. It stores the order book and matching logic on-chain, then routes filled trades to Drift's perpetual swaps for settlement. While innovative, Swift faces a limitation: when these matched trades need to be settled on Solana Layer 1, they are subject to typical network conditions and can experience delays or contention (especially in volatile markets with many active exchanges). Enter Raiku: DEXs like Drift can deploy specialized edge computing extensions for their trading engines. In this extension, orders can be matched on-chain (in the extension) with microsecond precision and settled instantly, significantly faster than Solana's 400ms block time. Using a Light SVM optimized for trading, this extension can achieve thousands of operations per second (e.g., matching bids and asks, updating trader positions) with deterministic scheduling. Crucially, using Raiku's guaranteed inclusion feature, once a trade is matched, it can be scheduled for settlement on the next Solana block without any uncertainty. No more racing against the clock or hoping for a trade to be credited; trade settlement is reserved and confirmed in advance. Payments and Fintech Infrastructure (e.g., "Stripe-like" payments, Squads): Payment applications require both high throughput and high reliability. Imagine a scenario like Stripe on Solana, a service that processes thousands of transactions per second for merchants, payroll, micropayments, and more. On Solana Layer 1, this is theoretically possible (given the high TPS), but in practice, if the network is congested or a single process in a payment process hogs too many computational units, other processes may crash. Using Raiku, one can create a payment extension, essentially a dedicated (edge ​​computing) zone for payment transactions. This extension can be optimized for simple token transfers and include a specialized/optimized environment or lightweight SVM for maximum efficiency. Through Raiku's bandwidth reservations, payment operators (such as stablecoin issuers or CBDC platforms) can continuously reserve throughput, for example, 500 TP/s, to ensure their transactions always flow smoothly, regardless of external demand. Users sending funds receive instant confirmation (no transaction delays). For enterprise or institutional use, Raiku can enable private settlement networks on Solana: "private settlement channels between major financial institutions with deterministic finality and encrypted flows." Imagine large banks settling foreign exchange or securities trades on a shared Solana blockchain. They could have their own Raiku extension, with transactions visible only to the parties involved (encrypted but still verifiable) and guaranteed finality. This would unlock use cases like cross-border payments, remittances, or interbank settlements on a public blockchain, while providing the predictability of SWIFT or FedWire. On the consumer side, tools like SquadX (a popular Solana multisig and coordination tool) could use Raiku to ensure that multisig transactions (which may involve multiple instructions) are executed reliably even during peak network times. A major pain point for DAO treasuries or multisigs is trying to execute a complex transaction and having it fail due to network issues. Raiku eliminates this problem by allocating a dedicated time slot after all signatories have approved it, allowing multisig transactions to complete atomically.

Furthermore, through RFQ-like liquidity integration, Raiku can enable new payment models: for example, decentralized payment applications can query market makers through Raiku's RFQ system to obtain the best exchange rate for currency swap transactions, all within a single extension without slippage or MEV. Similar to how Stripe routes payments through various banks to optimize fees and success rates, Raiku allows cryptocurrency payments to be routed to various liquidity sources in a controlled and deterministic manner. DeFi protocols and services (beyond trading): Beyond exchanges, many DeFi protocols can benefit from Raiku’s edge computing environment. Lending platforms can use them to perform instant liquidations through dedicated channels (Raiku even enables “automated risk management with microsecond precision,” meaning lending protocols can monitor positions and execute liquidation transactions within guaranteed time windows, reducing bad debts). Options and derivatives platforms can use edge computing zones as coordination hubs to orchestrate complex, multi-leg strategies across platforms, with Raiku ensuring that all legs execute atomically within continuous timeframes. For example, an options decentralized exchange (DEX) can ensure that when a user rolls over (closes one option position and opens another), both trades execute consecutively, ensuring uninterrupted price fluctuations. This level of control is currently only possible in centralized systems. Stablecoin issuers can also benefit: imagine USDC using a Raiku extension to prioritize its high-volume minting/redemption process, ensuring that large redemptions don't clog the network or get front-runnered. By allocating block space, they can maintain smooth operation even during periods of stress. Hybrid CEX/DEX and Institutional Access: Raiku has the potential to blur the line between centralized exchanges and DeFi. Its architecture allows for a model similar to a "regulated DeFi zone," where only KYC-verified entities (such as institutions) participate, ensuring compliance while still settling on Solana. The website hints at a "hybrid centralized/decentralized exchange with a superior user experience," where liquidity is provided by a centralized exchange (or consortium), but trades are settled on a public network extension. Leveraging edge computing, such a platform could offer the speed of a centralized exchange (a matching engine on dedicated hardware) with the transparency and custodial advantages of DeFi (settled on Solana). Non-financial Applications (Real-time Gaming, Social, AI Agents, IoT): While Raiku's initial focus appears to be on DeFi and financial markets, the framework has a wide range of applications. For real-time games or virtual worlds, Raiku can support extensions that handle rapid game state updates (imagine a fast-paced game running entirely on-chain; Raiku's scheduling mechanism ensures that operations complete within a timely tick system). For social networks or messaging apps on Solana, Raiku can provide the throughput to handle spikes in activity (for example, a popular post generating thousands of replies can be handled by extensions without overwhelming the main chain). The website's use cases explicitly mention "AI agents, DePIN, social networks, payment infrastructure, capital markets, high-frequency trading, and the internet on Solana." This broad vision suggests that Raiku sees itself as capable of supporting any high-demand application that today might find blockchains unable to scale or handle. For example, AI agents performing dozens of on-chain operations per second (such as participating in auctions, rebalancing portfolios, and so on) could leverage Raiku to reliably handle these operations. DePIN (a project similar to Helium or a decentralized Uber/Airbnb concept) typically requires a large number of microtransactions and device interactions, and Raiku can ensure that IoT devices have reserved throughput to continuously record data or settle payments.

What all of these cases have in common is that Raiku achieves a level of performance and trust that is unattainable with a single Layer-1. It gives developers a broader vision, allowing them to imagine on-chain services that rival the responsiveness of centralized servers.

Raiku’s Innovative Approach: Separation of Execution and Consensus for Predictable Performance

Raiku introduces a new block-building architecture that improves Solana’s base layer from within. It does this by enabling programmable coordination, giving validators and applications more deterministic execution without the need for a separate consensus mechanism or fragmented environment.

We can imagine the Raiku network as a specialized execution layer that runs in parallel with Solana’s main chain, driven by the same validator set, and can be joined by simply running the Raiku Sidecar. This frees up intensive application logic from competing for all of Solana's traffic, but Raiku isn't an isolated island and is tightly synchronized with Solana to ensure finality and data availability. Unlike other ecosystems still struggling with modularity tradeoffs or fragmented execution models, Solana provides a high-performance, monolithic base layer. It offers sub-second block times, ultra-low fees, and parallel execution via Sealevel. These features make it the blockchain most likely to implement concepts like just-in-time (JIT) or ahead-of-time (AOT) block auctions at scale without incurring high latency. While Solana achieves fast block times, low fees, and parallel execution via Sealevel, the current block construction process still has limitations: Validator profitability remains volatile, heavily dependent on native inflation subsidies and occasional MEV spikes.

  • Transaction inclusion is unpredictable, especially for applications requiring atomicity, deterministic ordering, or pre-confirmation.

  • Extra-protocol coordination is emerging, with protocols like Jito stepping in to address the lack of strong auction mechanisms and bundled execution guarantees.

    • What’s missing is a programmable coordination layer that can provide more consistent revenue opportunities for validators, reliable execution capabilities for dApps, and plug into Solana’s native architecture without compromising its low-latency, high-throughput characteristics.

    Solana’s incentive and economic framework is evolving rapidly, guided by a series of key Solana Improvement Documents (SIMD). These proposals reshape core validator incentives, transaction prioritization, and reward distribution, laying the foundation for a more competitive and robust ecosystem:

    • SIMD-0096: Redirect 100% of priority fees to validators, significantly increasing validator profitability and discouraging off-network transactions. SIMD-0123: Introducing a native, scalable protocol mechanism for validators to distribute rewards directly to stakeholders, thereby enhancing economic consistency and transparency. SIMD-0228: A dynamic, market-driven issuance schedule would be introduced, adjusting the inflation rate based on staking participation to improve economic efficiency and security. (However, the proposal failed to reach the required two-thirds supermajority in the March 2025 vote and has not yet been initiated.) While these changes focus on aligning incentives, they also increase competition among validators, prompting them to seek new sources of external revenue, which is particularly important during bear markets when MEV and priority fee opportunities decrease. The continued evolution of Solana's market structure lays the foundation for Raiku's orchestration engine, which is designed for reliable, predictable, and high-performance decentralized execution. The benefits of this separation of execution and consensus are profound. First, predictable block inclusion: While users' transactions may typically linger in the memory pool or queue, hoping to be included in the next block (or discarded due to a surge in load), Raiku is designed to provide hard guarantees regarding block inclusion and timeliness. Transactions submitted through Raiku receive "early" block inclusion confirmations, effectively reserving them for inclusion in upcoming blocks. This is achieved through Raiku's novel scheduling and auction mechanisms (which we'll discuss later). For users and dApp developers, this means no more spamming transactions or anxiously waiting for key transactions to be mined; you can know within milliseconds whether your transaction will be scheduled for a specific timeframe in the future. This focus on deterministic and predictable execution is a key differentiator. Traditional L2 on Ethereum can improve fees and throughput, but they generally cannot guarantee exactly when transactions will arrive on L1 (especially for optimistic rollups with challenging timeframes). In contrast, Raiku provides timeframe guarantees on Solana, a L1 platform known for its 400ms block times. Raiku essentially extends Solana with a "global scheduler" that applications can leverage to reserve block space. Another major advantage is fault isolation. On a monolithic L1, if a single application (say, a popular NFT minting program) suddenly consumes excessive resources or crashes, it could potentially cause the entire chain to degrade or halt. We've already seen this on Solana, where the workload of a single dApp could bring down the entire network. With Raiku, applications can run in isolated execution zones (also known as edge computing). If one zone encounters a problem—for example, a runaway program consuming excessive computation—it doesn't directly block Solana's main chain or other zones. The failure is contained to that extended environment. Solana's consensus remains unaffected, and other extensions continue to function normally. This fault isolation is similar to having multiple "sandboxes" on the network: each application (or group of applications) can use a dedicated slice of capacity and even have customized parameters without jeopardizing overall stability. Crucially, Raiku's architecture preserves the shared goals of both L1 and L2: security and sovereignty. Each Raiku scaling environment maintains execution sovereignty, meaning application developers can customize the execution logic, virtual machine, and parameters to their needs (in this sense, it's their "own chain") without having to build a new set of miners or validators from scratch. Raiku leverages a validator network that works in sync with the Solana validator set. In effect, Raiku validators will be Solana validators who choose to run the Raiku software (a sidecar for the validator client) and may earn additional fees for doing so. This means professional and robust security from day one, with a team of experienced validators at the helm, without the need to stake tokens solely for security. By decoupling consensus (which remains Solana's PoH/PoS mechanism responsible for finalizing blocks) from execution (which is handled by Raiku's scheduling and validator network), throughput is significantly improved. Solana no longer needs to execute every instruction of every program itself; it can outsource the execution of certain programs to Raiku extensions and only need to verify the results or proofs.

      In summary, Raiku is neither a standalone L1 nor a typical L2, it is Solana’s execution layer that introduces:

      • (a) separation of execution and consensus (freeing applications from the throughput limitations of L1),

      • (b) predictable inclusion and scheduling (no more probabilistic mempool games), and,

      • (c) strong fault isolation (a problem with one extension does not threaten the whole)

      It transforms Solana from a single-layer network to a multi-layer system: a base layer for consensus and global state, and upper layers for high-performance application-specific execution. Inside the Raiku Tech Stack: Deterministic Settlement and Modular Execution To deliver on its promise, Raiku introduces several novel components and transaction types. These components and transaction types can be thought of as infrastructure building blocks that work together to enhance Solana. Let's analyze the key elements of the Raiku tech stack: 1. Early Block Auctions and Inclusion Signals: At the core of Raiku is a radically new approach to blockspace management. Instead of an ad hoc, first-come, first-served mempool model, Raiku implements a slot auction market. Applications or users can bid in advance for upcoming slots on Solana (or, more precisely, for the scheduling coordinated by Raiku), ensuring their transactions receive priority. Winning bidders receive an "inclusion signal" in advance, essentially guaranteeing that their transaction (or bundle of transactions) will be included in a specific future block or sequence of blocks. These auctions are stake-weighted and atomic, meaning that scheduling follows Solana's distribution of stake (validators with more stake have greater capacity to include reserved transactions, thus aligning incentives), and transactions can be reserved in bundles and executed sequentially without interruption. As a result, Raiku users can now receive confirmations faster because their execution "ticket" is already guaranteed. Compare this to the traditional experience: on Ethereum, you send a transaction and hope miners pick it up as quickly as possible (perhaps raising fees if you're desperate), or even on Solana, you might send multiple transactions to ensure one gets picked up during periods of congestion. With Raiku, the entire process is like reserving a train seat in advance, without the jostling on a crowded platform. This system significantly reduces transaction failure rates and uncertainty, and one of Raiku's core goals is to guarantee transaction execution. Streaming Proofs: Unlocking Large Payload Execution with Sequential Blockspace A fundamental limitation of Solana today is the strict block data constraints designed to guarantee fast block propagation. For applications that need to submit large state updates, such as settlement engines or ZK rollup proofs, this can become a bottleneck. Raiku addresses this problem with sequential block space reservation, a concept enabled by its Ahead-of-Time (AOT) block auction model. By reserving space for a sequence of upcoming blocks, applications can reliably transmit large proofs or payloads in smaller, verifiable chunks without hitting Solana's per-block capacity limit. The idea is to split large transactions or proofs into smaller chunks that can be streamed and verified over multiple time slots, thereby bypassing Solana's strict per-block data limits. In practical terms, this means that applications can submit very large state updates or proofs (for example, zero-knowledge proofs or batches of hundreds of transactions) through Raiku, which will feed this data into Solana in chunks that validators can process. Instead of submitting large transactions that risk failure or bloat, applications can schedule and transmit structured data across multiple slots, while validators process and verify it in a controlled manner. 2. Fast and Deterministic Settlement ("Guaranteed Execution"): Many of the next-generation applications that Solana is designed to support, such as high-frequency trading platforms, real-time gaming systems, and institutional payment networks, require strict guarantees that transactions will land exactly when and where they are expected. In these areas, execution uncertainty isn't just a user experience drawback; it's a detriment to transactions. Unpredictable network congestion and transaction pool dynamics can cause transactions to fail, be reordered, or be delayed. For advanced use cases like automated liquidations, synchronous asset swaps, or arbitrage strategies, this unpredictability can lead to missed opportunities and capital inefficiencies. Raiku addresses this issue by guaranteeing transaction inclusion with both Ahead-of-Time (AOT) and Just-in-Time (JIT) reserved slots. For example, a bot operating based on real-time price fluctuations might prefer JIT inclusion, while an external system might opt ​​for a scheduled AOT slot. In both cases, users pay for the precision of time and bandwidth (using a combination of Raiku tokens and SOL). When a guaranteed inclusion transaction is submitted through Raiku, it is assigned a reserved execution window, ensuring it is processed at the scheduled time and won't be lost or reordered due to validator behavior or network congestion. While only slot leaders can include transactions, all validators running the Raiku sidecar propagate and confirm the transaction schedule in advance. Raiku uses a pre-consensus scheduling system to coordinate trade schedules, which are then executed by slot leaders during block production. By reserving block space in advance and assigning deterministic execution slots, Raiku mitigates peak failure scenarios, which have historically seen failure rates exceeding 90% for high-frequency users on Solana. It provides guaranteed bandwidth, precise latency, and predictable settlement even during periods of extreme load. The guaranteed execution mechanism is also designed to introduce MEV resistance. Because trades are scheduled and confirmed across the entire network in advance, front-running is reduced and sandwich attacks are mitigated by incorporating expected value extraction into the auction mechanism itself. Private order flow exchanges, which previously operated outside the protocol, are no longer necessary. Instead, order flow is transparently incorporated through a fair scheduling auction or reservation system. 3. Global Account Model and Unified State: One of Raiku's most groundbreaking aspects is its global account module. This component (scheduled to launch in V2 along with streaming proofs) addresses the state fragmentation problem head-on. The idea is to allow users and applications to maintain a unified identity and state across multiple execution environments. In practice, users will still have a single Solana wallet/address for the main Layer 1 and any Raiku extensions that interact with it. Assets and data can move seamlessly between the main chain and extensions without the need for traditional "bridges." The global account model will enable composability across extensions, so two Raiku extensions can interoperate or access shared user state as needed. This is a significant difference from typical Layer 2 systems. In a typical L2 system, each rollup is like a walled garden, requiring a bridge to transfer assets, and account/contract addresses may be chain-specific. Raiku's extensions, on the other hand, are more like "zones" within the Solana ecosystem, so the user experience remains consistent. Developers can deploy within the extension environment and still easily integrate with native Solana programs or accounts. For example, thanks to unified accounts, orders placed in a Raiku-powered order book extension can be settled to the user's main Solana wallet or recognized by programs on Layer-1. Technically, this can be achieved by having Raiku share Solana's account address space and signature verification, or by establishing a mechanism for synchronizing state roots between the extension and Layer-1. The end result is a solution to the state fragmentation problem, resulting in a single, combined state encompassing multiple execution environments. As the Raiku team explains, this enables composability across scaling environments, something neither Ethereum's Layer-2 (all operating independently) nor early Solana Rollup attempts can achieve. This is a first-principles approach designed to ensure that scaling doesn't lead to a fragmentation of the user base or liquidity. The global account module also supports multiple virtual machines. Raiku isn't limited to Solana's native virtual machine (SVM); it can host different VMs under the same coordination framework. In fact, Raiku is designed to support EVM-compatible extensions, allowing Ethereum projects to deploy their Solidity code as Solana extensions. The mention of "projects like Arbitrum Orbit will be deployed on Solana" suggests that Ethereum Layer 3 or custom chains can effectively connect to Solana through Raiku. This is significant: it means Ethereum dApps can benefit from Solana's performance and user base without abandoning its codebase. This suggests that Raiku has a somewhat hierarchical node architecture: ordinary users/applications communicate with an Ackermann node (or cluster), which then interacts with the validators to schedule execution. This is an intriguing design that scales input processing capacity and ensures the system can handle bursts of transactions by efficiently distributing transactions across validators.

      Summary

      The advent of Raiku marks a turning point for Solana and blockchain architecture as a whole. It showcases a vision: decentralized networks capable of achieving the reliability, speed, and flexibility once the exclusive domain of Web2 clouds or traditional financial systems. By introducing a coordination engine with deterministic execution, Raiku enables Solana to transcend the label of "just another L1" and become a true platform for mission-critical, high-performance applications.

      Consider what this means for developers: with Raiku, building on Solana feels like building on a scalable cloud service.

      • Need more throughput? Simply launch an extension and reserve the required slots. Need custom execution logic or a different virtual machine? Plug it in as an edge compute zone. Worried about the user experience during peak hours? Guaranteed transaction inclusion is provided so users never see a failed transaction again. The developer experience is significantly improved. Developers can build with confidence, knowing the infrastructure will meet their application's needs, not the other way around. The Solana base chain acts as a stable foundation, while Raiku provides the flexible, programmable scaffolding to help them push the boundaries. This has the potential to attract not only crypto-native developers, but also Web2 developers with strict performance requirements. They can use Solana without worrying about network slowdowns. In fact, Raiku could make Solana the platform of choice for any application requiring decentralization and high throughput. At the institutional level, Raiku may be the key to unlocking serious enterprise adoption of Solana. Banks, hedge funds, gaming companies, and social media startups—all of these can finally build on the chain with confidence, knowing the performance and control they need are already available. Solana has already become one of the more institutionally friendly blockchains (with companies like Jump investing in its technology); Raiku's promise of fine-grained execution control and high reliability further enhances Solana's appeal. Institutional-grade means 99.999% uptime, transaction deadlines, essential privacy protections, and regulatory compliance hooks—all of which Raiku can deliver (through isolated scaling, scheduling, and more). The journey has just begun; Raiku is currently in testnet, with a mainnet launch planned for late 2025, but the infrastructure and solutions are already in place. It addresses the pain points we listed at the beginning: scaling limitations (addressed by increasing modular throughput), MEV exploits (minimized through predictable ordering and auction mechanisms), re-staking challenges (avoided in a more controlled manner), fragmented state (addressed by global accounts), and performance issues (addressed by ensuring inclusiveness and fault isolation). Therefore, Raiku is a holistic solution, not a piecemeal fix.

data.title: The market has shifted from "brainless optimism" to wait-and-see attitude. What will we watch for the next round of growth?
data.description:
Author: WolfDAO

I. Core Judgment and Market Expectations

The core contradictions in the recent macro market focus on the Federal Reserve's "policy split" and "information vacuum"—two factors that both cause short-term market volatility and create structural benefits for long-term trends.

The macro cornerstone is clear: The market consensus is that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates by another 25 basis points at its October meeting. Powell's previously signaled "flexible dovishness" has effectively anchored expectations for a short-term rate cut, creating room for further easing policies.

Direct impact on the crypto market:

  • Liquidity drives up prices: Expectations of interest rate cuts have strengthened the market's confidence in "loose liquidity". Capital has poured into the crypto market in pursuit of high returns, pushing Bitcoin to a short-term high of $126,000, confirming the positive market sentiment;

  • The value of scarcity is further consolidated: continued loose policies will dilute the purchasing power of fiat currency, and Bitcoin, as an anti-inflation, non-sovereign value storage tool, has strengthened its long-term narrative - the current correlation between Bitcoin and gold is higher than that of US stocks, which is a direct reflection of this "hedge attribute".

2. Market consolidation under the policy “information fog”: not a logical failure

The current correction of Bitcoin from $126,000 to $121,000 and the wide fluctuation of altcoins are essentially the normal reactions of the market to digest high risks and wait for confirmation of macroeconomic data, rather than a breakdown of the previous macroeconomic logic.

III. The practical impact of policy game and market psychology

The current "policy split" and "information fog" of the Federal Reserve have directly led to intensified short-term market consolidation and amplified volatility. This can be broken down from two aspects:

1. Dovish consensus provides "emotional support"

Powell's flexible stance, coupled with the mainstream tendency within the Federal Reserve for "preventive interest rate cuts", has formed the market's psychological expectation of "long-term liquidity easing" - this is the core "emotional support" that enables the current crypto market to maintain a high range. As long as the Federal Reserve doesn't explicitly rule out the possibility of a rate cut, the market's fear of a "bear market" won't be fully ignited. 2. "Information Vacuum" Triggers the Release of Risk Premiums Due to the government shutdown, the release of key economic data was delayed, rendering the October Fed meeting a "fly in the dark" decision. This uncertainty translates into an "excessive optimism premium" during market rallies and an "uncertainty risk premium" once sentiment cools. The current market pullback is a phased release of this risk premium. 4. Market Psychological Transition and Strategic Outlook: Consolidation Accumulates Momentum for the Next Upward Movement The current market consolidation is essentially a natural transition in investor sentiment from "blind optimism" to "cautious wait-and-see"; it's not a trend reversal, but more likely a buildup of momentum for the next upswing. Shifting Focus and Key Trend Confirmation: The market's core focus has shifted to "delayed key economic data"—if indicators like employment and inflation show economic weakness after the data is released, it will provide "factual support" for the Fed to cut interest rates. At that time, pent-up liquidity will be released in a concentrated manner, and Bitcoin will likely maintain its strong momentum and hit new highs again. In short, the ultimate formation of crypto market trends depends on the combined verification of "macro data + Fed actions."
